title: Between Worlds
prompt: Extreme wide shot of a lone figure lying face-down in a vast cracked golden wheat field under a bruised violet sky, a worn backpack beside them, distant smoldering ruins of a village on the horizon, fantasy painterly style with warm amber sunset light and ash drifting in the air, color palette of gold, violet, and charcoal.
narrative: Alex wakes face-down in dead wheat. The air tastes of ash. A backpack lies within reach. The laptop inside still boots. The smartphone shows 47% battery. No signal. No towers exist here. On the horizon, a village smolders.
narration: The wheat is dry and warm against his face. Ash drifts through the air. It smells of burned sugar and something else. Something animal. Alex pushes himself up. His palms are scraped. The sky is too large here. The sun hangs low and amber, bigger than it should be. He reaches for the backpack. The laptop boots on the first try. The screen shows a familiar desktop. The smartphone reads forty-seven percent. No signal. He checks again. Still nothing. Not roaming. Not searching. There are no towers here because this is not where towers exist. On the horizon, a line of thin smoke rises from a cluster of low shapes. Buildings. Or what remains of them. He stands. His knees hold. He walks toward the smoke.

title: Broken Refuge
prompt: Wide shot of Alex walking through a crumbling stone village gate, short dark hair and weathered canvas jacket, backpack over one shoulder, holding up a glowing smartphone to scan damaged thatch-roofed cottages along a cracked cobblestone path, golden hour light casting long shadows, fantasy painterly style in warm earth tones.
narrative: The village gate is half-fallen. Alex steps over the rubble. The phone's camera identifies structural damage in three buildings. Someone has carved a word into the gate arch with a knife. REFUGE. The letters are uneven. They took a long time.
narration: The gate stones are warm to the touch. Cut recently or struck recently. The edges are clean. He steps through the gap. Cobblestones beneath his boots. Cracked in places. Missing in others. He holds the phone up. The camera sweeps across the cottages. The overlay highlights fractures. A collapsed beam. A shifted foundation. He reads the damage reports and does not like them. Above the gate, someone has carved a word into the stone. The letters are uneven. Deep. They were cut with a knife and patience. The word is REFUGE. Below it, smaller. He has to lean close to read it. STILL HERE.

title: The Elder's Door
prompt: Over-the-shoulder shot from behind Alex looking at Elara the village elder, silver-gray hair pulled back in a tight bun and a dark wool cloak, standing in a low doorway with arms crossed, her weathered face lined with suspicion, dim interior light behind her, fantasy painterly style with muted earth tones and warm interior glow.
narrative: Elara stands in the doorway of the largest cottage. She is tall and thin. Silver-gray hair pulled tight. Arms crossed. She looks at the smartphone in Alex's hand the way one looks at a held blade. He tells her he can help the village. She asks with what.
narration: She is older than the doorframe. The lines around her mouth cut deep. Silver-gray hair pulled back so tight it stretches the skin at her temples. A dark wool cloak, fraying at the hem. Her eyes go to the phone first. Not with wonder. With suspicion. The way a farmer eyes an unfamiliar dog. Alex holds it up. The screen glows. He explains what it can do. She does not interrupt. When he finishes, she asks one question. Her voice is low. Rough. Like rope dragged over stone. How many villages has he saved. He says none. She nods as though she expected that answer. Then she steps aside. Not an invitation. A challenge. The doorway smells of wood smoke and dried herbs. He walks inside.

title: Dead Soil
prompt: Medium shot of Alex kneeling in a barren field outside the village, short dark hair and canvas jacket dusty, holding a smartphone over cracked dry soil while Elara stands behind him with arms crossed, withered remains of failed crops visible, harsh midday sun, fantasy painterly style with desaturated ochre and dusty brown palette.
narrative: The fields are dead. Alex presses the phone's sensor against the soil. The AI reads nitrogen depletion, alkaline pH, no mycorrhizal activity. Three seasons of failure. Elara watches from behind. She has seen strangers promise before. He tells her the soil can be fixed.
narration: The soil cracks under his knees. Dry. Powdery. No earthworms. No roots. He presses the phone flat against the ground. The sensor reads what his eyes cannot. Nitrogen is gone. The pH is alkaline. There is no fungal network in the dirt. Three seasons minimum of failure. Maybe four. He catalogs the numbers. Calculates. The AI suggests a rotation plan. Composting ratios. Water table depth. It lays the answers out in clean columns. He looks up. Elara stands ten feet back. Her arms are crossed. Her jaw is set. She has watched strangers make promises before. Her eyes say so. He puts the phone away. He stands. He tells her the soil can be fixed. She asks how long. He says one season for partial recovery. Two for a real harvest. She turns and walks back toward the village without answering.

title: Strange Light
prompt: Low angle shot looking up at Alex sitting at a makeshift table at night, short dark hair illuminated by a glowing laptop screen, a small portable solar panel charging beside it, wires and salvaged materials spread out, three village children watching from behind a wooden fence in the background, fantasy painterly style with cool blue screen glow against warm candlelight.
narrative: At night, Alex works by laptop light. The solar panel charges from the day's sun. He runs calculations on irrigation paths using the village's one water source. Three children watch from behind a fence. They have never seen electric light before. He turns the screen so they can see.
narration: The laptop casts a blue square on the table. The solar panel hums beside it. Soft. Barely audible. It pulls in what the sun left behind. Alex traces irrigation paths on a digital map. The village has one water source. A spring, half a mile east. Currently feeding nothing useful. He models the flow rates. Gravity does most of the work. Pipes do the rest. The software lays it out in colored lines. Green for the path. Red for the loss points. He does not hear the children until he looks up. Three of them. Standing behind the fence. Their eyes are wide. They stare at the screen. The light reflects in their pupils. None of them speak. The youngest is holding a wooden figure. A horse, carved badly. Her knuckles are white around it. He does not shoo them away. He turns the screen slightly so they can see better.

title: First Water
prompt: High angle shot looking down at Alex ankle-deep in mud directing water through newly dug irrigation channels, villagers digging where he marks with stakes and string, Elara watching from the field edge with arms at her sides, green sprouts visible in foreground furrows, morning light casting long shadows, fantasy painterly style with fresh greens and golden tones.
narrative: The channels carry water from the spring to the fields. Alex stands in mud directing the flow. Villagers dig where he marks. Elara watches from the edge. Her arms hang at her sides. Not crossed. The first green sprouts break the surface in the nearest furrow.
narration: The water moves faster than he expected. It fills the first channel and spills into the second. The soil drinks it. He can almost hear it. The villagers dig where his marks show. Stakes and string. Simple geometry. They work in silence mostly. A man named Torren asks if this will really grow food. Alex tells him to check in two weeks. The man nods and keeps digging. Alex stands in the mud. His boots are ruined. His jacket is streaked with clay. The phone in his pocket buzzes a low-battery warning. He ignores it. At the edge of the field, Elara stands alone. Her arms hang at her sides. Not crossed. She watches the water move through the channels he designed. She does not smile. But she does not leave. In the nearest furrow, something green pushes through the mud. Small. Thin. Alive.

title: What the Fields Remember
prompt: Close-up of Elara's weathered face, silver-gray hair pulled back, deep lines around her eyes, a single tear track on one cheek, her mouth slightly open in quiet astonishment, warm golden harvest light on her skin, well-defined human nose and natural human facial features, fantasy painterly style.
narrative: Weeks later, the fields are green. Elara kneels at the edge of a row of growing grain. She runs her fingers through the stalks. Her mouth moves. Alex cannot hear what she says. Her hands are shaking. He turns away. Some moments are not his.
narration: The grain reaches her knee. Golden-green in the late light. Elara kneels in the dirt at the edge of the field. Her cloak pools around her. She runs her fingers through the stalks the way one touches a child's hair. Slow. Careful. Her lips move. No sound comes out. Or if it does, the wind takes it. Her hands shake. Not from age. From something else. The field smells of green and wet earth. Two months ago, this was dust. Now it grows. Her fingers find a grain head. Almost ripe. She cups it in her palm. Holds it the way one holds something fragile. Alex stands thirty feet behind her. He does not approach. Some moments belong to the people who lived through the worst of it. He turns and walks back toward the irrigation pump. It needs checking. There is always work. The sun warms the back of his neck.

title: The Robes
prompt: Medium close-up of Alex and Elara standing side by side at the village edge, Alex in his dusty canvas jacket with a laptop bag over his shoulder, Elara in her dark wool cloak with her chin raised, facing three robed figures approaching on the road, late afternoon light with long shadows, fantasy painterly style with deep amber and ominous purple tones.
narrative: The wizards arrive three days after the harvest. Three of them. Robed, staffed, certain of their authority. They come to claim the village's water rights under an old covenant. Elara meets them at the gate. Alex stands beside her. She does not cross her arms.
narration: They come on foot. Three figures in long robes, each carrying a staff carved with symbols the phone cannot translate. The lead wizard is tall. Shaved head. His robes are clean. Unreasonably clean for a dirt road. Elara watches them approach from the gate. Her chin is up. Her shoulders are square. She does not move when they stop. The lead wizard speaks first. His voice carries the weight of someone used to being heard. He names the water rights. The village has violated a covenant. The spring belongs to the Arcanum. Elara does not flinch. Alex stands one step behind her. His hand rests on the laptop bag. Not a weapon. But not nothing. The wizard's eyes flick to Alex. They narrow. He asks what manner of man stands without a staff or blade. Alex says nothing. Elara answers. He is the man who made the fields grow.

title: The Demonstration
prompt: Medium shot of Alex setting his laptop on a wooden table in the village square, the screen displaying a glowing irrigation map with colored data overlays, the lead wizard standing across from him with staff lowered slightly, villagers gathered around watching in silence, dramatic late afternoon light with dust motes in the air, fantasy painterly style with electric blue and warm amber contrast.
narrative: The wizard demands proof. Alex opens the laptop. The screen glows with irrigation models, soil data, and harvest projections. Numbers that speak louder than any spell. The wizard stares at the screen. His staff lowers. He asks what the tool wants in return. Alex says nothing. Tools do not want.
narration: Alex sets the laptop on the table. It looks wrong here. A flat glowing thing on rough wood. He opens the irrigation model. The screen fills with colored lines and numbers. Green channels. Blue flow rates. Yellow yield projections. He turns it so the wizard can see. The wizard stares. His staff lowers an inch. The numbers are not magic. They are math. The soil composition. The water table. The predicted yield per acre. The numbers do not care about robes or staffs or covenants. The wizard asks what school taught him this. Alex tells him it is not a school. It is a tool. The wizard looks at the screen for a long time. The villagers are silent behind him. Even the children. The wizard's grip on his staff loosens. He asks what the tool wants in return. Alex says nothing. Tools do not want. They work or they do not.

title: Silicon Fields
prompt: Extreme wide shot of the thriving village from above, green irrigated fields surrounding thatch-roofed cottages with new solar panels on rooftops, Alex and Elara standing together at the village center as small figures, a distant mountain range under a clear golden sky, fantasy painterly style with rich greens, warm golds, and soft blue sky.
narrative: A season later, the village has a name again. Crops fill every field. Solar panels catch the sun from four rooftops. Children carry water through clean channels. Alex and Elara stand at the center. She holds a tablet now. Hers. She is better at the math than he is.
narration: The village has a name now. Elara carved it herself into the gate arch. She spelled it carefully. It sits above the word REFUGE. The fields stretch in every direction. Green and gold. The irrigation channels hum with moving water. On four rooftops, solar panels tilt toward the sun. They charge batteries Alex built from salvaged parts and one very patient AI assistant. Children run through the paths between cottages. They carry small buckets. Their feet are muddy. They do not mind. At the center of the village, Alex and Elara stand side by side. She does not cross her arms anymore. She holds a tablet now. Hers. He taught her how to use it. She is better at the crop rotation math than he is. The sun is warm. The air smells of growing things. The gate reads REFUGE, STILL HERE, and now beneath it in careful letters. HOME.
